refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting across the communication chain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command injection to sensor feedback reception) under agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect p95 percentile across representative operational scenarios. Validate against 150 ms threshold with safety margin analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and transmit vehicle state telemetry with bounded latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive and execute operator commands with bounded latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall manage network communication to meet end-to-end latency targets.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect and respond to latency degradation or communication loss.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency communication protocol with priority queuing and adaptive compression; minimize middleware layers; co-locate telemetry acquisition and transmission in vehicle firmware.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing node (vehicle-mounted) to pre-process telemetry and buffer commands; use standard network stack with QoS tagging.
DP-C: Establish dual-path communication (primary + failover) with latency monitoring and automatic switchover; implement client-side prediction and dead-reckoning to mask network jitter.
DP-D: Use commercial VPN/remote desktop solution with standard TCP/IP; rely on network infrastructure tuning and operator training to manage latency perception.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-C hybrid: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol with priority queuing and adaptive compression (DP-A core) augmented by dual-path communication with latency monitoring and client-side prediction (DP-C resilience). This balances deterministic latency control with fault tolerance and safety criticality.

potential_sources: Coupling between telemetry acquisition rate (FR-NGHE-008.1) and network bandwidth availability (external constraint).
Coupling between command execution latency (FR-NGHE-008.2) and vehicle actuator response time (vehicle-level constraint).
Coupling between latency monitoring (FR-NGHE-008.4) and network link observability (depends on Network Link interface).
coupling_check.notes: Hybrid DP decouples latency control (dedicated protocol) from resilience (dual-path), reducing risk of over-specification. Adaptive compression and prediction are independent mechanisms that can be tuned separately.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports dual-path routing and QoS tagging. Risk: If not available, fallback to single-path with enhanced monitoring.
Assumption: Vehicle firmware can support low-latency protocol stack. Risk: Legacy vehicle platforms may require gateway hardware.
Assumption: Tele-operator client can implement prediction/dead-reckoning without introducing control instability. Risk: Requires validation testing with representative operators.
Assumption: p95 latency target is achievable given agreed network conditions. Risk: If network conditions are worse than assumed, target may be unachievable; requires explicit network SLA.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology) and obtain explicit network SLA. Define latency budget allocation across vehicle firmware, network, and tele-operator client. Specify acceptable prediction/dead-reckoning error bounds for safety validation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end latency control, including command reception, transmission, telemetry acquisition, network communication management, and latency monitoring. Encompasses vehicle-side communication firmware, tele-operator client software, and network protocol logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state telemetry (position, orientation, sensor readings, operational status) transmitted from Teleoperation module to tele-operator client and Safety Services for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) received by Teleoperation module from tele-operator client and routed to Vehicle control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (WAN, LTE, satellite, or hybrid) managed by Teleoperation module for command and telemetry transmission with latency monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Latency degradation alerts and communication loss notifications transmitted from Teleoperation module to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own latency control logic; latency is a cross-cutting concern that must be localized in Teleoperation module. High diffusion risk if latency logic scattered across multiple modules.

Vehicle owns actuator control and some telemetry acquisition, but does not own network communication or tele-operator client logic. Latency control requires coordination across vehicle and remote client; partial containment only.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency control. Owns communication protocol, command/telemetry routing, network management, and latency monitoring. Minimal diffusion risk; all latency-critical logic can be localized here.
Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but does not own latency control mechanisms. Latency monitoring output (SafetyAlert) is consumed by Safety Services, not owned by it.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because it naturally encapsulates the end-to-end communication path, network protocol logic, and latency monitoring. All sub-FRs (telemetry transmission, command reception, network management, latency detection) map cleanly to Teleoperation responsibilities. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimize coupling with other modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle firmware may not support low-latency protocol stack, requiring gateway hardware or protocol translation layer. Mitigation: Validate vehicle platform capabilities early; design protocol abstraction layer if needed.

Risk: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient observability (latency metrics, QoS status) for monitoring. Mitigation: Establish explicit network SLA and monitoring contract with network provider.

Risk: Tele-operator client (remote software) may be owned by different team/vendor, creating coordination risk. Mitigation: Define clear interface contracts and latency budget allocation; establish joint testing protocol.

Risk: Safety Services may require real-time latency data for safety decisions, creating tight coupling. Mitigation: Use SafetyAlert interface for asynchronous notifications; avoid synchronous latency queries.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit ownership model for tele-operator client software (if not already owned by Teleoperation team). Define latency budget allocation across vehicle firmware, network, and client. Create detailed interface specification for Network Link (latency metrics, QoS status, failover triggers). Validate vehicle platform support for low-latency protocol stack.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (hybrid low-latency protocol + dual-path with prediction) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. DP is specific, measurable, and traceable to FR. Hybrid approach balances deterministic latency control with fault tolerance, appropriate for high safety criticality. No logical gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Low-latency protocol logic could be scattered across vehicle firmware, network gateway, and tele-operator client if ownership boundaries are not enforced. Mitigation: Teleoperation module owns protocol specification and implementation across all layers.

Diffusion risk: Latency monitoring logic could be duplicated in Teleoperation and Safety Services if not carefully partitioned. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns monitoring; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert output only.

Diffusion risk: Adaptive compression algorithm could be implemented in multiple places (vehicle, network, client). Mitigation: Centralize compression logic in Teleoperation module; expose as configurable parameter.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides strong containment for DP. All protocol logic, network management, and latency monitoring can be localized within module boundaries. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimize coupling. Ownership is clear and unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module provides clear measurement points (command injection, sensor feedback reception). p95 percentile metric is standard and measurable. Safety margin analysis can be performed post-measurement.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency control. Sub-FRs (telemetry transmission, command reception, network management, latency detection) map cleanly to module responsibilities. No ambiguity in ownership or interface responsibilities.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit network SLA with latency, jitter, and packet loss specifications. Validate that agreed network conditions are achievable and documented.
Define latency budget allocation across vehicle firmware, network, and tele-operator client. Ensure sum of component budgets ≤ 150 ms with safety margin.
Create detailed interface specification for Network Link, including latency metrics, QoS status, and failover triggers.
Validate vehicle platform support for low-latency protocol stack. If not supported, design protocol gateway or abstraction layer.
Establish joint testing protocol with tele-operator client team (if external). Define latency measurement methodology and acceptance criteria.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (telemetry transmission, command reception, network management, latency detection) that must be individually designed and verified. Teleoperation module assignment is sound and well-justified, but sub-FR decomposition is necessary to allocate latency budgets, specify protocol details, and define monitoring logic. Proceeding to next decomposition level is appropriate.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and transmit vehicle state telemetry with bounded latency ≤ 50 ms (allocated budget).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall receive and execute operator commands with bounded latency ≤ 50 ms (allocated budget).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall manage network communication to meet end-to-end latency targets via low-latency protocol, adaptive compression, and dual-path routing.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall detect latency degradation (> 150 ms p95) and communication loss, and transmit SafetyAlert to Safety Services within 100 ms.

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports dual-path routing and QoS tagging.
Vehicle firmware can support low-latency protocol stack (or gateway hardware is available).
Tele-operator client can implement prediction/dead-reckoning without control instability.
Agreed network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) are achievable and documented.
p95 latency target of 150 ms is feasible given network conditions and component budgets.
Teleoperation module ownership is unambiguous and includes both vehicle-side and client-side logic.
Safety Services will consume latency alerts asynchronously via SafetyAlert interface.
open_risks: Network SLA not yet established; actual network conditions may be worse than assumed.
Vehicle platform support for low-latency protocol stack not yet validated.
Tele-operator client ownership and latency budget allocation not yet clarified.
Prediction/dead-reckoning error bounds not yet defined; control stability not yet validated.
Latency budget allocation across components (vehicle, network, client) not yet finalized.
Failover and recovery mechanisms for dual-path communication not yet specified.
evidence_needed: Network SLA document with latency, jitter, and packet loss specifications.
Vehicle platform capability assessment for low-latency protocol support.
Latency budget allocation spreadsheet with component budgets and safety margins.
Detailed interface specification for Network Link (metrics, QoS, failover triggers).
Tele-operator client design document with prediction/dead-reckoning algorithm and error bounds.
End-to-end latency measurement results under agreed network conditions (p95 percentile).
Safety validation report confirming control stability with prediction/dead-reckoning.
